ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic meatball food product class; no specific entry for Chinese deep-fried meatballs (干炸丸子). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chinese deep-fried meatballs. A0CKP (Meat products, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Chinese deep-fried meatballs. No suitable parent concept identified that would add ontological value beyond FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors already provided.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of deep-fried meatballs consists of approximately 6 to 8 pieces weighing around 150 g, commonly served as a shared appetiser or main dish component in a Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Pork meatballs, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; adapted with reference to USDA FDC ID 167903 (Pork, ground, 72% lean / 28% fat, raw) and FDC ID 168140 (Pork, cured, ham, extra lean, canned) for deep-fried pork meatball composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese deep-fried pork meatballs (干炸丸子) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried ground pork meatballs (FDC reference: Pork meatball, cooked, fried, composite). Values are consistent with published data for deep-fried pork meatballs: approximately 60–65% ground pork, 10–12% starch/binder, 5% egg, seasonings, and absorbed frying oil. Energy and macros cross-referenced against NCC Food and Nutrient Database category anchors for fried meat patties/balls.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-starch preparations — analogous estimate based on published GI values for fried meat patties and starch-bound meat products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese deep-fried pork meatballs (干炸丸子) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogous fried meat-starch composite foods in the database. The dominant carbohydrate source is starch binder (wheat/corn starch), which is partially gelatinised during frying; the high fat and protein content of the meatball matrix significantly attenuates glycaemic response. GL per 150 g serving = (45 × 11.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.8 (rounded), classified as low GL. This estimation is consistent with published literature on fried meat-starch composites.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~720 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of deep-fried meatballs due to the high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning, with a single 150 g serving providing approximately 720 mg sodium — nearly 31% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low GI (estimated 45), but high total fat and caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume deep-fried meatballs in moderate portions as the low estimated GI (~45) and high protein-fat matrix attenuate postprandial glucose response; however, the high caloric density (~285 kcal/100 g) and saturated fat content require portion control and infrequent consumption to support glycaemic and weight management goals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~6.8 g/100 g), high sodium (~480 mg/100 g), and significant cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g) from deep-frying and pork fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat deep-fried meatballs as an occasional food only, given the combination of high saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and sodium, all of which are established risk factors for atherosclerosis and elevated blood pressure per AHA/ACC dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Meatballs (干炸丸子, Gān Zhá Wánzi) are a classic Beijing dish with roots in the Manchu Han Imperial Feast tradition, representing one of the most iconic preparations of old Beijing cuisine. The dish consists of seasoned ground pork mixed with starch binder, egg, soy sauce, ginger, scallion, sesame oil, and white pepper, shaped into small balls and deep-fried in hot oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crispy while the interior remains tender and juicy. The name 干炸 (gān zhá) literally means 'dry-fried' or 'deep-fried without sauce', distinguishing this preparation from braised or sauced meatball dishes. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 285 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 20 g fat (including 6.8 g saturated fat), and 11.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high at approximately 480 mg per 100 g, making this a food of concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~45) due to the high protein and fat content attenuating starch digestion, making postprandial glucose impact moderate when consumed in controlled portions. This dish is commonly served as a shared appetiser or part of a multi-dish Chinese banquet meal. In Singapore, it is available at Chinese restaurants specialising in Beijing or northern Chinese cuisine. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or soy.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chinese deep-fried pork meatballs (干炸丸子) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central under this specific dish name. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable deep-fried ground pork meatball composites and cross-referenced with NCC category anchors. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar fried meat-starch products. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central deep-fried ground pork meatball composites; no exact dish-level entry exists in any primary regional database for 干炸丸子. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a future HPB/USDA exact match if one becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite reference used for this estimation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference data for fried pork meatball composites and are reported as best estimates rather than confirmed values. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for 干炸丸子 exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant source (See Ya Tonight 今晚有局) does not carry a confirmed halal certification; is_halal is set to false by default. 6. The dish name in the input record includes the restaurant brand prefix '姥姥家' (Lao Lao Jia, meaning 'Grandma's house'); the canonical food_name has been normalised to 'Deep-Fried Meatballs' with the brand prefix captured in aliases.
